name: Tries
tagline: Prefix trees — anything about words to prefixes.
concept:
## The idea

A **trie** (from re*trie*val, often pronounced "try") is a tree where each edge represents a character, and paths from root to node spell out prefixes. It's the data structure when you need to:

- Check if a word exists or if any word **starts with** a prefix — both in O(k) where k is the word length, instead of O(n·k) scanning a list of n words.
- Store a dictionary compactly when words share prefixes (think autocomplete).

In Python, the simplest trie is a **nested dict**: `trie = {}`, then `trie['c']['a']['t'] = True` marks "cat" as a word. For more structure, define a `TrieNode` class with a `children` dict (maps char → child node) and an `is_end` flag.

Insertion: walk/create nodes for each character, mark the last. Search: walk; if you hit a missing child or the path doesn't end with `is_end`, no match. Prefix check: just walk — you don't need `is_end`.

## Python you'll pick up here

- `collections.defaultdict(TrieNode)` — auto-creates children on first access, cleaner than `if char not in node.children`
- Nested dict tries: `trie = {}; current = trie; current = current.setdefault(char, {})`
- Class definitions with `__init__`, instance variables (`self.children`, `self.is_end`)
- The `any()` builtin and recursive backtracking for wildcard search
- DFS on a grid + trie pruning (Word Search II)
whenToUse: You're asked to store a dictionary and check prefix existence (autocomplete, word validation)
Search needs to scale with word length, not number of words
You need to find all words in a grid/string that match a dictionary (DFS + trie pruning)

problems.title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
problems.keyIdeas: The foundational trie problem. Each `TrieNode` holds a dict of children (char → node) and an `is_end` flag. Insert walks/creates nodes for each character and sets the flag. Search walks and checks the flag. `startsWith` walks but ignores the flag. All operations are O(k) where k is the word length.
hints: Each node needs to store: (1) its children as a map from character to child node, and (2) a boolean marking if a word ends here.
Insert: for each character, get-or-create the child node, then move to it. After the last character, set `is_end = True`.
Search is like insert but read-only: walk the path; if you can't find a character or the final node isn't marked as a word end, return False.

solutions.label: Brute force — hashset
solutions.keyIdea: Store all words in a set for search. For prefix check, iterate through all words checking if any starts with the prefix. Motivates the trie: prefix search is O(n·k) here vs O(k) with a trie.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) insert, O(1) search, O(n·k) startsWith where n = #words, k = prefix length

solutions.label: Optimal — TrieNode with dict
solutions.keyIdea: Define a TrieNode with a children dict and is_end flag. Walk/create for insert, walk/check for search. All operations O(k). Reach for this when you need fast prefix queries and memory isn't the bottleneck — the dict is flexible for any character set.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(k) per operation, k = word/prefix length
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(n·k) total, n = number of words

solutions.label: Alternative — TrieNode with array[26]
solutions.keyIdea: Use a fixed array of 26 children (for lowercase 'a'-'z') instead of a dict. Faster lookups (O(1) array index vs dict hash), more cache-friendly, but wastes space when nodes are sparse. Reach for this in performance-critical code with known character sets.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(k) per operation, k = word/prefix length
solutions.spaceComplexity: O(n·k·26) = O(n·k) total, but higher constant — each node allocates 26 slots

problems.description: A trie (pronounced as "try") or prefix tree is a tree data structure used to efficiently store and retrieve keys in a dataset of strings. There are various applications of this data structure, such as autocomplete and spellchecker.

problems.title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure

video.title: NeetCode — Design Add and Search Words Data Structure (Python)

problems.keyIdeas: Extends the basic trie with **wildcard search**: `.` matches any character. When you hit a `.`, branch: try *all* children at that position (DFS/backtracking). If any branch completes the match, return True. Insert is unchanged from Trie I.
hints: The wildcard `.` means you can't just walk a single path — you need to explore all possible branches at that character.
Write a recursive helper `dfs(node, i)` that searches from `node` starting at index `i` of the word. If `word[i] == '.'`, recurse on all children.
Base case: when you've consumed the whole word (i == len(word)), check if the current node is a word end.

solutions.label: Brute force — list + manual wildcard matching
solutions.keyIdea: Store all words in a list. For search with wildcards, iterate through every word and match character-by-character, treating '.' as a wildcard. O(n·k) per search, motivates the trie's ability to prune branches early.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(1) addWord; O(n·k) search where n = #words, k = word length

solutions.label: Optimal — Trie + DFS for wildcards
solutions.keyIdea: Insert is standard trie. Search becomes a recursive DFS: on '.', try every child; on a letter, follow that one path. The trie prunes invalid paths immediately (no need to scan all words), making it much faster than brute force when the dictionary is large.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(k) addWord; O(m) search worst-case for m nodes explored (exponential with many dots)

problems.description: Design a data structure that supports adding new words and finding if a string matches any previously added string.

problems.title: Word Search II
problems.keyIdeas: Build a trie from the word list. DFS from every cell, carrying the current trie node. Each step checks if `board[r][c]` is a child of the current node — if not, prune that path (no word can match). When you hit a node with `is_end`, you've found a word. Mark cells visited in-place (or use a set) to avoid revisiting. This is O(rows·cols·4^L) where L is max word length, but the trie pruning makes it vastly faster than running Word Search I per word.
hints: Running the grid DFS separately for each word would be O(words · rows · cols · 4^L). A trie lets you search for all words in a single pass.
Build the trie from the word list. Start DFS from every cell, passing the current trie node. At each step, check if the cell's letter is in node.children; if not, stop (prune).
When you reach a node where `is_end` is True, you've found a word — add it to the result. Remove it from the trie (set `is_end = False` and prune the node if it has no children) to avoid duplicates.

solutions.label: Brute force (Word Search I per word)
solutions.keyIdea: Run the classic backtracking Word Search once for each word in the list. Very slow when the word list is large.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(w · m · n · 4^L) where w = #words, m·n = grid, L = max word length

solutions.label: Optimal — Trie + DFS
solutions.keyIdea: Build a trie from words, then DFS from every cell carrying the trie node. Prune branches when the letter isn't in the trie.
solutions.timeComplexity: O(m · n · 4^L) where m·n = grid, L = max word length (but trie pruning reduces this dramatically)

problems.description: Given an m x n board of characters and a list of strings words, return all words on the board.
